description: Empirisch erhobene Routing-Tabelle für DESi. Jede Zelle ist (task_class, model, k) -> (score, mean_input_tokens, mean_latency_ms, cost_per_item_usd). Quelle: minimaltest-Serie auf LongMemEval-S (25 scorable items), SciFact (30 items), planted-bug code-review (15 items).

memory_recall.description: LongMemEval-S — multi-session conversation history, factual lookup. Tests retrieval + small-context-window pressure.

code_audit.description: Planted-bug code review — 15 distinct bug types in 9-module mini-codebase. Tests bug localization with scattered evidence.
code_audit.winning_state_note: retrieval HURTS — 40% recall on generic 'find bugs' question

cells.note: AVOID — worst score across all models on this task

scientific_claim.description: SciFact 3-way classification: SUPPORT / CONTRADICT / NEI. Tests retrieval + scientific reasoning with high question specificity.
scientific_claim.winning_state_note: retrieval recall is 100% on this benchmark, k=3 works well

cells.note: specializes in scientific text — strong here

provisional_models._description: Frontier-tier models from major providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google). NOT yet empirically measured on DESi's three task classes. The router refuses to dispatch to provisional models by default — they require calibration via the standard k-curve + cross-task sweep before being promoted to tasks.<task>.cells. Pricing is real (from OpenRouter as of June 2026).

anthropic/claude-opus-4.8.expected_role: max-accuracy escalation target for hardest cases

openai/gpt-5-nano.expected_role: cheapest frontier model; possible new default candidate

calibration_plan.step_1_pricing: Already done — exact prices fetched from OpenRouter API.
calibration_plan.step_2_classifier_compatibility: Verify each model accepts the OpenRouter chat-completion API with the system+user prompt format used by desi/answerer.py. Llama 3B remains the classifier; we are only testing as answerers.
calibration_plan.step_3_k_curve_per_model: Run minimaltest_model_sweep.py extended with these models on the same 25 scorable LongMemEval-S items at k = {3, 5, 8, 10}. Produces score per (model, k).
calibration_plan.step_4_per_task_scores: Run code_audit (15 bugs) and paper_audit (30 SciFact claims) with each new model on the winning state from existing measurements.
calibration_plan.step_5_promote_to_cells: Merge measured scores into tasks.<task>.cells with full (mean_in, mean_latency, cost) tuples. Update router_rules.default if the new measurements change the Pareto-cheapest.
calibration_plan.step_6_mixed_workload_validation: Re-run minimaltest_mixed_workload.py to confirm the extended routing table still wins vs naive_big (Granite 8B) and the new naive baselines (always-Sonnet, always-Haiku, etc.).

hypotheses_to_test: Does k* generalize across providers? Haiku 4.5 — k=3 like Granite Micro, or different?
Do frontier-tier models also have task specialties (like Qwen 7B catastrophic on code)?
Does cross-provider mix Pareto-dominate single-provider routing?
Is GPT-5-nano (\$0.05/M in) the new floor for routing default?

default.reason: Best absolute score 0.867 on this task

epistemic_specialties.ibm-granite/granite-4.0-h-micro: Code audit best-in-class; default low-cost router for memory tasks
epistemic_specialties.ibm-granite/granite-4.1-8b: Max score on 2 of 3 tasks (memory @k=10, scientific claims); 10x more expensive than micro
epistemic_specialties.meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct: Pareto-cheapest winner on 2 of 3 tasks; under-rated cost-optimal default
epistemic_specialties.qwen/qwen-2.5-7b-instruct: Strong on scientific text (0.90), catastrophic on code (0.37) — task-heterogeneity ratio 2.4x
epistemic_specialties.meta-llama/llama-3.2-3b-instruct: Fastest latency (0.7s) but weak scores across all tasks
epistemic_specialties.mistralai/ministral-3b-2512: Solid all-rounder but Pareto-dominated by Granite Micro and Llama 8B

open_questions: Does this routing table generalize across more task classes (multi-hop, tool-use, creative)?
What is the k* for Llama / Qwen / Mistral on Code-Audit and Paper-Audit specifically (we only used the Granite-winning state)?
Is there a structural signature (model architecture / training mix) that predicts k* and task specialty?
How does the router behave under task-class uncertainty (when the input doesn't cleanly belong to one bucket)?
Confidence signals: can we read self-uncertainty from logprobs / verdict-confidence and use that for escalation?
